---
title: "Homework 1: Data Science Foundations, Sequencing Formats, QC, and HIV/TB Data Extraction"
author:   100 Points Total
date: Due May 29, 2026
output: pdf_document
---

This assignment covers the foundational material from the first few weeks of class: the data science process, sequencing technology and file formats, quality control, and the very first step of the HIV/TB RNA-seq pipeline -- downloading the raw reads from SRA and inspecting them with FastQC/MultiQC. All questions are multiple choice and will be administered as a Canvas quiz.

Please save your code/notes for each part of the assignment. Your instructor may request it.

Submit your completed assignment via the designated homework (Quiz) submission site on Canvas.

## Course Foundations

\begin{enumerate}

  \item Which of the following best describes \emph{structured} data?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Free-text clinical notes
    \item Data organized into rows and columns with a predefined schema (e.g., a gene-by-sample counts matrix or a relational database table)
    \item Raw FASTQ sequencing reads
    \item Microscopy images
  \end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

## Sequencing Technology

\begin{enumerate}
  \setcounter{enumi}{1}

  \item Illumina short-read sequencing is based on which chemistry?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Sanger chain-termination with capillary electrophoresis
    \item Nanopore translocation of single DNA strands through a protein pore
    \item Sequencing by synthesis (reversible terminator nucleotides imaged after each cycle)
    \item Pyrosequencing on bead-immobilized templates
  \end{enumerate}

  \item Compared with single-end sequencing, paired-end sequencing has which of the following advantages? (Select the best answer.)
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Reads come from both ends of each fragment, giving better mapping in repetitive regions and an estimate of insert size
    \item It eliminates the need for adapter trimming
    \item It produces longer individual reads (kilobases instead of hundreds of bases)
    \item It requires no library preparation
  \end{enumerate}

  \item A study wants to comprehensively call protein-coding variants in 200 individuals on a tight budget. Which sequencing design is the most cost-effective fit?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Deep (30x) whole-genome sequencing
    \item Low-coverage whole-genome sequencing
    \item Long-read Nanopore sequencing of the entire genome
    \item Whole-exome sequencing
  \end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

## File Formats and Quality Scores

\begin{enumerate}
  \setcounter{enumi}{4}

  \item What is the key difference between a FASTA file and a FASTQ file?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item FASTA is binary; FASTQ is text
    \item FASTA contains aligned reads; FASTQ contains unaligned reads
    \item FASTQ stores a per-base quality score for every base; FASTA does not
    \item FASTQ is used only for proteins; FASTA is used only for nucleotides
  \end{enumerate}

  \item A base has a Sanger PHRED quality score of $Q = 30$. What is the corresponding probability that the base call is \emph{incorrect}?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item 1 in 1,000 (0.001)
    \item 1 in 100 (0.01)
    \item 1 in 10,000 (0.0001)
    \item 1 in 10 (0.1)
  \end{enumerate}

  \item In a standard FASTQ record, the four lines correspond (in order) to:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Sequence; quality string; identifier; separator
    \item Identifier; quality string; separator; sequence
    \item Quality string; sequence; identifier; pair flag
    \item @-prefixed read identifier; the nucleotide sequence; a `+' separator (optionally with the identifier); the per-base quality string
  \end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

## HIV/TB Data Extraction and QC

\begin{enumerate}
  \setcounter{enumi}{7}

  \item You will download the 33 FASTQ files for the HIV/TB dataset from SRA using \texttt{sratoolkit}. The SRR accessions are listed in the \texttt{SRR\_Acc\_List\_hivtb.txt} file. Which of the following is the correct usage of \texttt{fastq-dump} to download a single accession and gzip-compress the output in one step?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item fastq-dump --compress gzip SRR12345678
    \item fastq-dump SRR12345678 --gzip-compress
    \item fastq-dump --SRR SRR12345678 --gzip
    \item fastq-dump SRR12345678 | gzip > SRR12345678.fastq.gz
    \item fastq-dump --gzip SRR12345678
  \end{enumerate}

  \item You want to download all 33 HIV/TB FASTQ files \emph{in parallel} on Amarel using a SLURM job array, then run FastQC on every file and aggregate the results with MultiQC. Which of the following statements about that pipeline is \emph{TRUE}?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item \texttt{\#SBATCH --array=0-32} creates 32 array tasks, and MultiQC re-runs quality control with stricter thresholds than FastQC.
    \item \texttt{\#SBATCH --array=0-32} creates 33 array tasks indexed 0 through 32, and \texttt{multiqc .} aggregates the per-sample FastQC reports into a single interactive summary.
    \item \texttt{\#SBATCH --array=33} creates 33 array tasks, and MultiQC trims adapters from the FASTQ files.
    \item \texttt{\#SBATCH --ntasks=33} is the correct directive for parallel array downloads, and MultiQC aligns reads to a reference genome.
  \end{enumerate}


  \item Go ahead and download all of the HIV/TB FASTQ files into your home directory or scratch directory on Amarel. How large is the gzipped \texttt{SRR6305400.fastq.gz} file (as reported by \texttt{ls -l} / \texttt{ls -lh})?
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item 412,387,629 bytes ($\sim$393 MB)
    \item 3,521,476,108 bytes ($\sim$3.4 GB)
    \item 956,264,153 bytes ($\sim$912 MB)
    \item 1,243,889,201 bytes ($\sim$1.2 GB)
  \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
